title: Homemade Urad Dal Pappad

overview.description: Thin, crisp Indian pappad made from seasoned urad dal flour, rolled thinly and dried before cooking.

ingredients: ½ tsp freshly ground black pepper
method: Mix the urad dal flour, salt, black pepper, asafoetida and cumin seeds in a bowl.
Add the water gradually, mixing until a very stiff dough forms.
Add the oil and knead firmly for 8–10 minutes until the dough is smooth and elastic.
Cover and rest the dough for 30 minutes.
Divide the dough into approximately 20 small portions.
Roll each portion into a very thin disc, approximately 12–15cm in diameter.
Use as little dry flour as possible while rolling.
Arrange the discs in a single layer on clean trays or baking paper.
Dry them thoroughly in a warm, dry place until completely hard and brittle.
Turn the discs occasionally during drying to ensure they dry evenly.
Once completely dry, store in an airtight container.
To cook, heat a small amount of oil until hot.
Fry one pappad at a time for a few seconds until it expands and becomes crisp.
Alternatively, cook in a dry frying pan or under a hot grill, turning as necessary.

learning.did-you-know: Pappad are made in many different regional styles using pulses such as urad dal, moong dal and chickpeas, with different spices and textures.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How pulse flour can be turned into a very stiff dough, rolled extremely thin and preserved by drying.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A longer cooking project that transforms urad dal flour into a traditional Indian accompaniment and provides a useful introduction to drying food for storage.
chapman-tips: The dough should be much stiffer than chapati or roti dough.
Roll the discs as thinly as possible; thickness is the biggest factor affecting the finished texture.
Use very little flour when rolling because excess flour can affect the texture and drying.
Dry the pappad completely before storing. Any remaining moisture can lead to mould.
A pasta machine may help produce consistently thin discs, although the dough may require experimentation to find the right setting.
If the pappad puff unevenly during cooking, they are still perfectly usable.
Frying produces the most dramatic texture, while dry-cooking produces a lighter result.
notes.batch-cooking: This recipe is designed to be made as a batch. Once completely dry, the uncooked pappad can be stored in an airtight container and cooked as needed.
notes.leftovers: Cooked pappad are best eaten immediately, but uncooked dried pappad can be stored and cooked later.
